name: Justice Defenders
mission: Defending justice with defenceless communities in Africa through legal education, training, and practice.
theoryOfChange: Justice Defenders has a published 'Theory of Change' as part of their 'The Justice Defenders Way' document on their website. This document outlines their approach, values, and 2025-2027 strategy. Their approach involves working inside prisons to provide legal education, training, and practice, aiming to transform prisons into places of learning and service and build a movement that reimagines justice from the inside out. They explicitly state, 'Our 2025-2027 strategy will grow our movement of defending the defenceless. Over the next three years, we will shift power within more prisons and continue to reshape justice systems from the inside out. We will expand legal education and practice, create new pathways from incarceration to advocacy, and advance relational systems change that restores trust across justice institutions.' An external case study also confirms that Justice Defenders developed a 'powerful theory of change' through workshops and facilitated sessions.

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions a robust 9.2 months of working capital as a strength, but the 'Financial Health' component score is only 1/7 due to concerns about spending exceeding revenue. This discrepancy should be acknowledged in the rationale.
The rationale does not mention the governance concerns related to CEO compensation being a high percentage of revenue, which is a significant factor contributing to the low governance score (0/10).

score_details.score_summary: Justice Defenders (USA) shows moderate alignment and moderate impact (minor risk deductions applied).

zakat.claim_evidence: There is no explicit mention of "zakat" on the provided information about Justice Defenders (USA) or its donation methods. The organization is described as a US nonprofit working in Africa, and while it accepts donations, there is no indication of a dedicated zakat fund or campaign by the US entity. (Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Justice_Defenders)

all_citations.claim: Justice Defenders has a Charity Navigator score of 91.0/100 and 9.2 months of working capital.
The organization reported total revenue of $1,331,729 and a program expense ratio of 58.5%.
The organization focuses on legal education and training, resulting in law degree graduates and prisoner releases.
Justice Defenders provides legal support and education to defenseless communities.
The organization trains paralegals and supports individuals in their release from prison.
The charity provides direct legal support and facilitates law degree completion for beneficiaries.
The organization accepts general donations and is categorized as Sadaqah-eligible.

dimension_explanations.alignment: Justice Defenders addresses the urgent cause of legal advocacy and human rights in underserved regions [3]. As a Sadaqah-eligible organization, it provides a pathway for general charitable giving, though it currently has a low donor fit for those seeking specific religious alignment in their philanthropy [8].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization focuses on capacity building by training paralegals and facilitating law degrees for incarcerated individuals [3][7]. While it demonstrates effectiveness in legal outcomes, such as prisoner releases and degree completions, its program expense ratio stands at 58.5% [1][2]. Financial health is supported by a robust 9.2 months of working capital [1].

baseline_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders (USA) is a non-profit organization dedicated to defending justice within defenseless communities in Africa by providing legal education, training, and practice [3][4]. The organization operates with a mission to equip prisoners and prison officers with the skills to provide legal services to those without representation [5].

However, only <cite id="8">58.5% of expenses</cite> are currently categorized as direct program costs.
impact.improvement: Impact efficiency could be improved by <cite id="22">increasing operating reserves</cite> to at least 3 months of expenses to mitigate the risks of revenue volatility.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: The charity provides detailed annual reporting on <cite id="5">releases and graduates</cite>, though it lacks independent RCT-level evaluations.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: By training prisoners and staff as paralegals and lawyers, the charity creates an internal legal service that bypasses the shortage of external counsel, leading to faster trials and fair releases.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence of legal releases confirms that the training programs translate into tangible freedom for the defenseless.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: The organization is currently spending <cite id="6">significantly more than its annual revenue</cite>, indicating a high need for new funding to sustain operations.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="3">prison-based paralegal training model</cite> and focus on <cite id="9">Alternative Dispute Resolution</cite> within African correctional facilities.

strengths.detail: The organization maintains a <cite id="7">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, driven by a <cite id="11">100/100 score in Culture & Accountability</cite>.
Justice Defenders has successfully facilitated the release of <cite id="5">over 15,000 individuals</cite> and trained <cite id="12">47 law degree graduates</cite> within the prison system.
The charity demonstrates strong fiscal discipline in donor acquisition, spending only <cite id="13">$0.12 to raise every $1</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: Justice Defenders' primary strength lies in its unique 'inside-out' model of legal empowerment. By training <cite id="14">370 paralegals</cite> within the prison population, they create a sustainable infrastructure for justice that does not rely solely on external lawyers. This approach has led to <cite id="15">11,250 clients released</cite> as documented in their 2022 reporting. Furthermore, the organization maintains <cite id="16">9.2 months of working capital</cite>, ensuring operational stability even during revenue fluctuations, such as the <cite id="17">-13.8% three-year revenue CAGR</cite> observed recently.
rich_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders is a specialized legal advocacy organization that empowers incarcerated individuals and prison staff in Africa through <cite id="1">legal education, training, and practice</cite>. Founded in <cite id="2">2007</cite>, the organization addresses the critical gap in legal representation by training paralegals and lawyers within the prison system to provide <cite id="3">direct legal support to those without counsel</cite>. Operating primarily in <cite id="4">Kenya, Uganda, and Gambia</cite>, the charity has demonstrated significant scale, reporting that <cite id="5">15,338 prisoners were released</cite> in 2024 alone. Financially, the organization reported <cite id="6">total revenue of $1,868,837</cite> in 2023, maintaining a <cite id="7">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>. While its program expense ratio of <cite id="8">58.5%</cite> is lower than some peers, its model focuses on high-leverage capacity building, such as <cite id="9">facilitating 2,032 releases through Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR)</cite>. The organization holds a <cite id="10">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>, reflecting a high level of public disclosure regarding its operations and outcomes.
